_meta.null_signal_reason: Multiple indicator domains in Amber to Red across growth, inflation, financial stability, and trade. Regime change evidence present. Middle East conflict escalation and Supreme Court tariff ruling create new structural tensions.

stress_regime_preliminary.regime_change_evidence: US effective tariff rate now 13.7 percent highest since 1941 excluding 2025 rates. Peak consumer tariff pressure forecast for April to October 2026. Middle East conflict since March 1 disrupts energy supply via Strait of Hormuz. Oil prices near 108 USD per barrel. ECB projects Q2 2026 headline inflation spike to 3.1 percent. EM capital flows show outflows exceeding pandemic highs. Fitch downgraded US credit rating to AA plus. Gold reserves now exceed Treasury holdings in global central bank reserves for first time in 30 years.

indicator_domains.signal: US ISM Manufacturing PMI at 52.7 in March 2026, continuing expansion but underlying components show rising input cost pressures. Yale Budget Lab projects unemployment increase of 0.3 percentage points by end 2026. CBO forecasts real GDP growth 2.2 percent in 2026, down from pre-tariff expectations. Market probability of US recession by end 2026 stands at 29 percent per Polymarket.
indicator_domains.key_data_point: ISM Manufacturing PMI 52.7; Yale unemployment projection plus 0.3pp; Recession odds 29 percent
indicator_domains.source: ISM, Yale Budget Lab, CBO, Polymarket

indicator_domains.signal: 5-year breakeven inflation rate rose 26 basis points since Middle East conflict onset to highest level since February 2025. Consumer sentiment fell 6 percent in March to lowest since December 2025 with year-ahead inflation expectations climbing from 3.4 percent to 3.8 percent. ECB projects headline inflation spike to 3.1 percent in Q2 2026 from Middle East energy shock. Federal Reserve divided with 12 of 19 FOMC members predicting at least one rate cut while 7 expect none. Market implies 80 percent probability Fed holds rates through 2026. Powell signals new energy supply shock incoming.
indicator_domains.key_data_point: Breakeven inflation plus 26bps; consumer inflation expectations 3.8 percent; ECB Q2 projection 3.1 percent; OECD forecasts US headline inflation 4.2 percent in 2026 vs December forecast of 3 percent
indicator_domains.source: FRED, University of Michigan, ECB, Federal Reserve, OECD, Powell speech

indicator_domains.signal: US CMBS delinquency rate increased 38 basis points to 6.2 percent in March 2026. Private credit funds imposed 5 percent redemption limits as of April 2 2026. Foreign Treasury holdings fell to lowest level since 2012 reflecting reserve drawdowns and diversification. Chicago Fed NFCI at minus 0.43 as of March 27, indicating improving financial conditions but with recent volatility spike around Iran conflict. Fitch downgraded US sovereign credit rating from AAA to AA plus on April 1. Gold reserves at 4 trillion USD vs Treasury holdings at 3.9 trillion USD signals reserve composition shift.
indicator_domains.key_data_point: CMBS delinquency 6.2 percent; private credit redemptions capped 5 percent; Fitch downgrade to AA plus; Gold exceeds Treasuries in CB reserves
indicator_domains.source: S&P Global, Bloomberg, Treasury TIC, Chicago Fed, Fitch Ratings, BIS

indicator_domains.signal: One year after Liberation Day April 2 2025, US effective tariff rate stands at 12 percent, down from 22.5 percent peak but five times higher than pre-2025 levels. US goods trade deficit fell 24 percent year-over-year through February 2026. US imports from China down 28 percent in 2025 alone and 40 percent below 2018 levels. Supreme Court struck down emergency tariffs in February 2026 forcing Trump administration to impose 15 percent Section 122 tariffs effective April 2026 for up to 150 days. Trump announced investigations March 2026 into China, Vietnam, Taiwan, Mexico, Japan, EU under Section 301. EU threatened reciprocal tariff retaliation if Trump imposes 10 to 25 percent tariffs on eight European countries for Greenland dispute. Food prices forecast to increase 1500 USD annually per household from tariff impact. Trade professionals cite tariff volatility as top regulatory concern at 72 percent vs 41 percent one year prior.
indicator_domains.key_data_point: Effective tariff rate 12 percent; US-China trade deficit down 32 percent; Yale projects 1500 USD household food cost increase; 72 percent of trade professionals cite tariff volatility as top concern
indicator_domains.source: CFR, White House, Yale Budget Lab, PIIE, Peterson Institute, Thomson Reuters Global Trade Report

indicator_domains.signal: US dollar share of global foreign exchange reserves fell from over 70 percent in 2000 to 56.7 percent by end 2025. Chinese yuan touched 35-month high as dollar weakens amid Middle East conflict and capital reallocation. Central bank reserve managers show 95 percent expect global gold reserves to continue rising through end 2026 with zero percent intending to reduce holdings, first time in recorded history. De-dollarization movement accelerated since Russia sanctions in 2022 with BRICS nations and other economies seeking trade currency alternatives. Brazil reduced dollar share of reserves to 72 percent record low while doubling gold holdings.
indicator_domains.key_data_point: USD reserve share 56.7 percent down from 70 percent in 2000; CNY at 35-month highs; 95 percent of CB managers expect gold holdings to rise; Brazil dollar reserves at 72 percent record low
indicator_domains.source: IMF COFER, World Gold Council, Bloomberg, Federal Reserve, BIS

indicator_domains.signal: US federal deficit projected at 1.9 trillion USD in fiscal 2026 growing to 3.1 trillion by 2036. Primary deficit 2.6 percent of GDP growing to 2.1 percent by 2036. Net interest payments rising to 4.6 percent of GDP by 2036 from 3.3 percent in 2026. Foreign holdings of US Treasuries at lowest level since 2012 testing demand for upcoming auctions. Corporate bond supply reached record 65 billion USD issuance in single day March 2026. Bond market liquidity worsened sharply in April 2025 after tariff announcement but recovered by early 2026 before recent deterioration post-Iran conflict. Treasury yield curve remains inverted signaling recession expectations. Japan 10-year JGB yield at 2.39 percent all-time high reflecting BOJ rate hike expectations.
indicator_domains.key_data_point: Federal deficit 1.9 trillion USD in 2026; net interest 4.6pp by 2036; foreign Treasury holdings lowest since 2012; 10Y yield curve inverted; Japan 10Y JGB 2.39 percent all-time high
indicator_domains.source: CBO, Treasury Department, Liberty Street Economics, Trading Economics, Bank of Japan

central_bank_tracker.last_action: Held rates 3.5 to 3.75 percent on March 18 2026
central_bank_tracker.forward_guidance: Powell warns of new energy supply shock. Divided FOMC dot plot shows 12 of 19 members expect at least one cut while 7 expect none. Market prices 80 percent probability of no change through 2026 end. Fed balance sheet reduction under discussion with goal of minimizing footprint while maintaining rate stability.

central_bank_tracker.last_action: Held rates unchanged in March 2026 pending Middle East conflict assessment
central_bank_tracker.forward_guidance: ECB projects Q2 2026 headline inflation spike to 3.1 percent due to energy shock from Middle East conflict. Staff projections show 2026 GDP growth 0.9 percent vs 1.5 percent in 2025. Signals readiness to hold steady through energy shock assessment window ending April. Consumer confidence softened in March.

central_bank_tracker.last_action: Signals likelihood of April 2026 rate hike pending conflict assessment
central_bank_tracker.forward_guidance: Former BoJ chief economist states April rate hike likely if conflict perceived as short-lived by end April. Bank will await Middle East developments before deciding. 10-year JGB yield at 2.39 percent all-time high reflecting market rate hike expectations. First tightening cycle globally if executed.

central_bank_tracker.last_action: Cut structural policy tool rates 0.25 percentage points on January 15 2026 with 500 billion yuan relending quota increase
central_bank_tracker.forward_guidance: Central Economic Work Conference signals moderate loose monetary policy for 2026. PBOC stated room to cut rates and reserve requirement ratio remains. China PMI data resilient in early 2026 but showing conflict spillovers with rising input costs and longer delivery times. Property crisis continues with 80 percent of developers estimated to exit market in coming years.

central_bank_tracker.last_action: Held Bank Rate at 3.75 percent in March 2026 after six cuts since August 2024
central_bank_tracker.forward_guidance: BoE signals Middle East conflict will push inflation higher than expected at least short-term. Current inflation 3 percent. Rate cuts on pause pending energy price evolution and conflict duration clarity. Inflation impact scale depends on war length and global energy supply disruption persistence.

tariff_tracker.measure: Reciprocal tariffs averaging 15 percent on most countries 10 percent on UK and other varied rates, plus Section 232 steel and aluminum duties
tariff_tracker.macro_impact_assessment: One year after Liberation Day April 2 2025, effective tariff rate now 12 percent five times higher than pre-Trump second term baseline. Yale Budget Lab estimates 0.6 percent consumer price increase in short run declining to near zero if Section 122 tariffs expire on schedule. Long-run GDP reduction 0.1 percent or 30 billion USD annually if tariffs expire, 0.2 percent if extended. Unemployment rises 0.3 percentage points by end 2026. Peak consumer pressure forecast April to October 2026 with 12 to 18 month lag estimated. Food cost increase projected 1500 USD per household annually. Manufacturing margins compressed by imported input cost increases. Supply chain complexity doubled year-over-year with 72 percent of trade professionals citing tariff volatility as top concern vs 41 percent prior year.

tariff_tracker.measure: Tariffs raised by 145 percentage points from January to April 2025 with 125 percent rate on China by April 10 2025, then raised to 15 percent Section 122 in March 2026
tariff_tracker.macro_impact_assessment: US imports from China dropped 28 percent in 2025 alone, 40 percent below 2018 baseline. US China goods trade deficit down 32 percent year-over-year through February 2026 and 46 percent from April 2025 through January 2026. For first time since 2000, China no longer has largest US trade deficit. Monthly exports to US fell to near zero in May 2025 after Xi Jinping restricted rare earth exports April 4 2025. Trade adjustment shows US replaced two-thirds of import gap with other suppliers while Chinese exporters cut prices average 8 percent to find alternative markets. China trade surplus expanded to record levels in 2025 despite US tariffs due to redirection toward non-US markets.

tariff_tracker.measure: Rare earth permanent magnet export restrictions imposed April 4 2025, initial pause on agricultural tariff retaliation late 2025, further talks scheduled into 2026
tariff_tracker.macro_impact_assessment: Xi initially imposed 10 to 15 percent tariffs on US agricultural exports and restricted rare earth mineral exports. Monthly shipments to US fell to near zero in May 2025. Restrictions later paused under series of agreements in late 2025. US auto industry briefly threatened with Chinese input cutoffs twice in 2025. Rare earth restrictions particular concern given AI chip dependency and defense industrial base implications.

tariff_tracker.measure: 25 percent tariff on all imports from Mexico and all Canada imports except oil and energy at 10 percent, issued February 1 2025 under IEEPA, later USMCA compliant goods exempted March 6 2025
tariff_tracker.macro_impact_assessment: Over 85 percent of Canada-US trade and 84 percent of Mexico-US trade remains tariff-free due to USMCA exemption. Supreme Court struck down IEEPA tariffs February 2026 forcing recalibration. Canada implemented 25 percent retaliatory tariffs on US steel and consumer goods. Canada-US auto exports fell 10 to 20 percent from tariffs. Mexico same dynamics with nearly 20 percent of US new vehicles sourced from Mexico and Canada combined making North American auto industry particularly vulnerable to tariff escalation. Cox Automotive notes 60 percent of US market volume would be affected by tariffs on Canada and Mexico with price increases certain to be passed to consumers.

tariff_tracker.target: European Union and specific European countries
tariff_tracker.measure: 10 percent tariff effective February 1 2026 rising to 25 percent June 1 2026 on eight European countries France Germany UK Netherlands Denmark Norway Sweden Finland unless Denmark agrees to sell Greenland to US
tariff_tracker.status: Announced with extraordinary EU diplomatic response
tariff_tracker.macro_impact_assessment: EU convened extraordinary meeting of diplomats and signaled potential freeze of July 2025 EU-US trade framework agreement in retaliation. Largest political bloc in European Parliament threatened to block framework agreement implementation if dispute escalates. EU could reintroduce previously adopted retaliatory tariffs on US goods including bourbon and motorcycles. Framework agreement tariff decreases subject to parliamentary and member state approval now at risk. Retaliatory tariff suspension scheduled to expire February 6 2026 providing automatic enforcement mechanism if EU chooses escalation. EU firms compete globally with US firms for markets making trade friction bidirectional damage.

tariff_tracker.target: China Vietnam Taiwan Mexico Japan European Union and dozens of other economies
tariff_tracker.measure: New Section 301 investigations announced March 2026 into allegedly unfair trading practices
tariff_tracker.macro_impact_assessment: Supreme Court February 2026 ruling against many Trump 2025 tariffs forced administration recalibration. New Section 301 investigation pathway creates extended uncertainty for targeted economies spanning Asia Europe and Americas. Investigations typically culminate in tariff or trade restriction recommendations. Extends tariff shock cycle beyond current visibility window.

tariff_tracker.measure: Potential or announced retaliatory tariffs: EU retaliatory duties on bourbon motorcycles, Canada 25 percent on steel and goods, India safeguards on steel and chemicals, Latin America and Africa increased tariffs on select sectors
tariff_tracker.status: Mostly Announced or Suspended pending US escalation
tariff_tracker.macro_impact_assessment: Global trade did not retract in 2025 despite tariff escalation with US imports rising 4 percent in real terms due to frontloading and AI equipment demand offsetting tariff effects. However McKinsey research shows tariffs triggered significant trade readjustment with EU facing double squeeze of Chinese import surge and US tariffs. ASEAN thrived increasing trade with both US and China. Retaliation chains create escalation risk if Trump administrations further increases tariff rates.

tail_risks.risk: Sustained Middle East conflict escalates to direct US military involvement causing oil price spike above 150 USD per barrel disrupting global energy markets and triggering stagflation across developed and emerging markets simultaneously
tail_risks.trigger_condition: Direct attack on US military assets or personnel in Middle East region or escalation of Strait of Hormuz disruption beyond current levels affecting 20 to 30 percent of global seaborne oil flows

tail_risks.risk: Section 122 tariffs made permanent by Trump administration instead of expiring after 150 days in June 2026, extending tariff shock into election cycle and causing long-run GDP reduction of 0.2 percent or 60 billion USD annually in 2026 dollars with unemployment rising additional 0.15 percentage points
tail_risks.trigger_condition: Trump administration signals intent to extend Section 122 authority or Congress grants permanent authority before 150-day expiration June 17 2026. Political calculation favors permanent approach if administration views tariff revenue as substitute for fiscal adjustment.

tail_risks.risk: Federal Reserve forced into emergency rate cuts due to financial stability stress despite inflation above target triggering dual mandate conflict and credibility damage to price stability anchor
tail_risks.trigger_condition: US CMBS delinquency exceeds 8 percent, CRE loan defaults spike, or private credit fund redemption pressures force mass selling of illiquid assets. Current CMBS delinquency 6.2 percent with 38 basis point March increase.

tail_risks.risk: Foreign central banks accelerate departure from US Treasuries as reserve asset causing auction failures and forced Fed balance sheet expansion contradicting stated goal of balance sheet normalization and constraining policy space
tail_risks.trigger_condition: Foreign Treasury holdings decline further below current 2012 lows, auction bidding ratios fall below 2.0x, or gold reserve allocation by central banks surpasses Treasuries by 10 to 15 percent marginal shift. Currently gold at 4 trillion USD vs Treasuries at 3.9 trillion USD with 95 percent of CB managers expecting gold to rise through 2026.

tail_risks.risk: EU escalates tariff retaliation and threatens NATO cohesion via decoupling from US security umbrella in response to Trump Greenland tariff threats and broader trade antagonism
tail_risks.trigger_condition: EU implements suspended retaliatory tariffs February 6 2026 expiration date and begins formal process of implementing 25 percent counter-tariffs on US goods. Simultaneous signals from EU on strategic autonomy and defense spending acceleration signal credible split.

tail_risks.risk: Emerging market capital outflows exceed pandemic highs causing currency crises in vulnerable EM economies and triggering emerging market debt default cycle via higher refinancing costs
tail_risks.trigger_condition: Capital Flows Monitor March 2026 shows outflows since Middle East conflict onset exceeded pandemic highs. Continued conflict persistence or widening of Strait of Hormuz disruption. Additional US tariff escalation particularly on Asia and Mexico could trigger reinforcing outflows.

tail_risks.risk: AI hyperscaler capex plans fail to materialize due to supply chain constraints semiconductor shortage or energy grid inability to support data center buildout causing abrupt narrative reversal and valuation compression in tech sector
tail_risks.trigger_condition: Goldman Sachs projects 527 billion USD 2026 AI capex. Deloitte notes 50 percent memory price spikes projected mid-year from hyperscale demand concentration. Any significant supply disruption or energy constraint would reveal capex unsustainability.

tail_risks.risk: Inverted yield curve persists through 2026 and recession odds exceed 50 percent forcing S&P 500 revaluation from current 7600 Goldman Sachs year-end target downward by 15 to 25 percent as rate cut probabilities repriced
tail_risks.trigger_condition: Yield curve remains inverted through April May and June 2026 quarters. Labor market shows additional cooling signals beyond current jobless claims and hiring volume declines. Two consecutive quarters of negative GDP growth would formalize recession declaration.

key_judgments.judgment: The April 2 2025 Liberation Day tariff shock has entered its post-litigation phase with new institutional uncertainty. Supreme Court February 2026 ruling struck down IEEPA tariffs forcing reliance on Section 122 time-limited authority expiring June 17 2026. Peak consumer impact from tariffs now forecast for April to October 2026 placing election-sensitive inflation pressure directly into 2026 campaign season.

Middle East conflict which commenced March 1 2026 has altered inflation expectations and central bank calculus across all major economies. Energy price shocks visible in 5-year breakeven inflation rising 26 basis points and ECB projecting Q2 2026 headline inflation spike to 3.1 percent. This creates policy divergence risk with BoJ signaling tightening while Fed remains on pause.

Central bank reserve composition shift evidenced by gold now exceeding Treasuries in global CB reserves for first time in 30 years signals fundamental reallocation away from dollar assets. Combined with de-dollarization movement accelerating since 2022 Russia sanctions and US dollar reserve share declining from 70 percent in 2000 to 56.7 percent by end 2025, this represents structural not cyclical shift.

Emerging markets are experiencing capital outflows exceeding pandemic highs according to Capital Economics March 2026 monitor. Combined with tariff targeting of EM exporters Vietnam Mexico and others, EM asset class faces synchronized pressure from US policy tightening and geopolitical shock. EM debt default risk rising despite better fundamentals in select countries.

US commercial real estate stress visible in CMBS delinquency rising 38 basis points to 6.2 percent in March 2026. Private credit redemption limits imposed April 2 2026 capping withdrawals at 5 percent signal liquidity management concerns. Combined with foreign Treasury selling and Fitch downgrade to AA plus, financial stability risks manifest across asset classes.

AI hyperscaler capex spending projected at 527 billion USD for 2026 is increasingly dependent on policy and supply chain assumptions. Goldman Sachs notes capex estimates have been consistently low and potential upside of 200 billion USD remains. However Deloitte warns of 50 percent memory chip price spikes mid-year from concentration risk. AI capex sustainability has become macro risk variable not just tech sector consideration.

Federal budget deficit projected at 1.9 trillion USD in 2026 growing to 3.1 trillion by 2036 with net interest reaching 4.6 percent of GDP creates long-term fiscal sustainability concerns. Combined with tariff revenue dependency and foreign Treasury selling pressure, fiscal policy flexibility has become constrained.

Market volatility and de-risking accelerated in March 2026 with S&P 500 down 5 percent and Russell 2000 down 5.17 percent. Goldman Sachs year-end target of 7600 now appears contingent on banking sector delivering strong Q1 earnings beats. Recession odds priced at 29 percent in Polymarket while yield curve remains inverted.
